answer: In chapter 13 of Traité de la propriété Vol I (work slug: charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99), Comte's political-economic argument is that the lower-basin populations are 'proprietors in undivided ownership' of the river that flows through their lands — and that mountain landowners, through deforestation, violate this shared property right. The argument runs as follows: forests on mountain slopes are the mechanism that regulates water infiltration; they slow the absorption of rain and snowmelt, ensuring rivers maintain a steady, usable flow. When upland proprietors strip their slopes of trees, they cause rivers either to dry up (through increased evaporation) or to become violent torrents (through unimpeded runoff). Either outcome causes 'considerable damage to the lower properties and to the populations that occupy the bottom of the basin.' The political-economic core is that private property rights are not absolute: a proprietor's use of his land is constrained by his obligation not to damage the property of others. Mountain owners cannot, under the guise of improving or exploiting their own land, destroy the natural infrastructure — the forest cover — on which downstream communities' agriculture, industry, and commerce depend. Comte marshals empirical evidence from Trinidad, Martinique, Saint-Domingue, Mauritius, the United States, and France (with testimony from Humboldt, Volney, and Arthur Young) to show this is not theoretical but a documented mechanism of economic ruin. The chapter thus argues for a legal-property principle: collective riparian rights of downstream populations must constrain how upland private owners can treat their land.
